content.text: Meshes as the Target Representation
A triangular mesh encodes 3D geometry as a set of vertices
and faces

(each face is a triple of vertex indices). Meshes are the universal format for real-time rendering, game engines, and 3D printing. Nearly every 3D generation pipeline ultimately targets a mesh — even methods that internally use NeRF, Gaussian splatting, or signed distance fields must export a mesh to be practically useful.
Two paths to mesh generation dominate:
implicit-to-explicit conversion
(extract a mesh from a learned implicit field via marching cubes) and
feed-forward direct generation
(a neural network outputs the mesh in a single pass, bypassing optimisation).

attrs.alt: 3D mesh generation pipelines: implicit-to-explicit via marching cubes (left) and feed-forward InstantMesh pipeline (right), with method comparison table

content.text: Marching Cubes: Extracting Meshes from Implicit Fields

Marching cubes (Lorensen & Cline, 1987) converts a scalar field sampled on a regular voxel grid into a triangular surface at a given iso-value. The algorithm processes each cubic cell independently: it classifies the 8 corner values as inside (field < 0 for SDF) or outside (field > 0), looks up the corresponding triangle pattern from a 256-entry table, and linearly interpolates the crossing positions on the cube edges. The result is a mesh that tightly approximates the zero-level set of the field.

# pip install scikit-image trimesh
import numpy as np
from skimage.measure import marching_cubes
import trimesh

def extract_mesh_from_sdf(
    sdf_fn,              # callable: (N, 3) → (N,) signed distance field
    resolution: int = 128,
    bounds: tuple = (-1.0, 1.0),
    iso_value: float = 0.0,
) -> trimesh.Trimesh:
    """
    Sample an SDF on a voxel grid and extract the zero iso-surface mesh.
    sdf_fn(pts): pts has shape (N, 3), returns SDF values (N,).
    """
    lo, hi = bounds
    lin = np.linspace(lo, hi, resolution)
    # Build 3D grid: (R, R, R, 3)
    grid_xyz = np.stack(np.meshgrid(lin, lin, lin, indexing="ij"), axis=-1)
    pts_flat = grid_xyz.reshape(-1, 3)

    # Evaluate SDF in batches to avoid OOM
    batch_size = 65536
    vals_list = []
    for start in range(0, len(pts_flat), batch_size):
        vals_list.append(sdf_fn(pts_flat[start : start + batch_size]))
    vals = np.concatenate(vals_list).reshape(resolution, resolution, resolution)

    # scikit-image marching_cubes expects density (positive inside)
    verts, faces, normals, _ = marching_cubes(vals, level=iso_value)

    # Convert grid indices back to world coordinates
    verts = verts / (resolution - 1) * (hi - lo) + lo
    mesh = trimesh.Trimesh(vertices=verts, faces=faces, vertex_normals=normals)
    return mesh


# Example: sphere SDF
def sphere_sdf(pts: np.ndarray, radius: float = 0.5) -> np.ndarray:
    return np.linalg.norm(pts, axis=1) - radius

mesh = extract_mesh_from_sdf(sphere_sdf, resolution=64)
print(f"Sphere mesh: {len(mesh.vertices)} verts, {len(mesh.faces)} faces")
print(f"Watertight: {mesh.is_watertight}")
mesh.export("sphere.obj")
Extracting a Mesh from a Trained Nerfstudio / NeRF Model

# After training a NeRF with Nerfstudio, extract a mesh:
# Option A — CLI (simplest)
# ns-export poisson-mesh --load-config outputs/<run>/config.yml \
#   --output-dir exports/ --target-num-faces 50000

# Option B — Python API for custom control
from nerfstudio.utils.eval_utils import eval_setup
from nerfstudio.exporter.marching_cubes import generate_mesh_with_multires_marching_cubes
import torch

config, pipeline, _, _ = eval_setup(
    config_path="outputs/<run>/config.yml",
    test_mode="inference",
)
pipeline.eval()

with torch.no_grad():
    mesh = generate_mesh_with_multires_marching_cubes(
        field=pipeline.model.field,
        resolution=512,             # higher = finer mesh, more memory
        iso_level=0.0,
        coarse_mask=None,
        output_path="exports/scene.ply",
    )
print(f"Exported mesh: {mesh.vertices.shape[0]} vertices")
3D Gaussian Splatting → Mesh via SuGaR
3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) represents scenes as unstructured clouds of 3D Gaussians — not directly meshable. SuGaR (Surface-Aligned Gaussian Refinement, Guédon & Lepetit 2023) extracts a mesh by:
Adding a regularisation term that encourages Gaussians to lie flat and close to the scene surface.
Extracting a Poisson surface reconstruction mesh from the regularised Gaussian centres.
Binding Gaussian primitives to the mesh faces, then refining the mesh further with a joint appearance loss.

# Install SuGaR (requires a trained 3DGS checkpoint first)
git clone https://github.com/Anttwo/SuGaR
cd SuGaR && pip install -e .

# Step 1: train a standard 3DGS model
python gaussian_splatting/train.py -s data/my_scene/ -m output/my_scene_3dgs/

# Step 2: run SuGaR regularisation + mesh extraction
python train.py \
  -s data/my_scene/ \
  -c output/my_scene_3dgs/ \
  -r "density"             # or "sdf" for sharper boundaries
# Outputs: output/refined_mesh/my_scene/sugarmesh_3Dgs7000_densityestim02_sdfnorm02.obj
Feed-Forward Mesh Generation: InstantMesh
InstantMesh (Xu et al., 2024) is the state-of-the-art open-source pipeline for single-image-to-mesh. It chains Zero123++ (multi-view diffusion) with a Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) that uses a
TriPlane NeRF
representation followed by
FlexiCubes
— a differentiable marching cubes variant that jointly optimises vertex positions and the scalar field during training, producing cleaner mesh topology than standard marching cubes.

# pip install huggingface_hub diffusers transformers accelerate pillow rembg
# Also needs: instant-mesh package from https://github.com/TencentARC/InstantMesh

import torch
from PIL import Image
from rembg import remove as remove_background
from huggingface_hub import snapshot_download

# --- Step 0: prepare the input image ---
img = Image.open("input_object.jpg").convert("RGBA")
img_nobg = remove_background(img)      # remove background; output has alpha
img_nobg.save("input_nobg.png")

# --- Step 1: Generate 6 multi-view images with Zero123++ ---
from diffusers import DiffusionPipeline, EulerAncestralDiscreteScheduler

pipe_mv = DiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "sudo-ai/zero123plus-v1.2",
    custom_pipeline="sudo-ai/zero123plus-pipeline",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
)
pipe_mv.scheduler = EulerAncestralDiscreteScheduler.from_config(
    pipe_mv.scheduler.config, timestep_spacing="trailing"
)
pipe_mv.to("cuda")

# The pipeline returns a 3×2 grid (960×640) of 6 views
mv_grid = pipe_mv(
    img_nobg.convert("RGB"),
    num_inference_steps=36,
    generator=torch.manual_seed(42),
).images[0]

# Split into 6 individual 320×320 views
def split_6_views(grid: Image.Image) -> list[Image.Image]:
    W, H = grid.size
    tw, th = W // 2, H // 3
    return [
        grid.crop((c * tw, r * th, (c+1)*tw, (r+1)*th))
        for r in range(3) for c in range(2)
    ]

views = split_6_views(mv_grid)
for i, v in enumerate(views):
    v.save(f"view_{i:02d}.png")

print(f"Generated {len(views)} views")

# --- Step 2: reconstruct mesh with InstantMesh LRM ---
# See: https://github.com/TencentARC/InstantMesh/blob/main/run.py
# The LRM encoder processes the 6 views and a TriPlane decoder produces
# a NeRF field, which FlexiCubes converts to a textured mesh.
# (Full LRM inference omitted — uses ~18 GB VRAM for the 3B model.)
print("Feed the 6 views to the InstantMesh LRM to get a .obj / .glb mesh.")
GET3D: GAN-Based Textured Mesh Generation

NVIDIA's GET3D (Gao et al., 2022) trains a GAN that directly generates 3D textured meshes — not a NeRF, not a point cloud, but a proper triangle mesh with a UV-unwrapped texture atlas. The generator maps a latent vector to a signed distance function via DMTet (Differentiable Marching Tetrahedra), extracts the surface, then synthesises a texture conditioned on the geometry. Because the rendering is differentiable, the GAN discriminator sees 2D renderings and provides image-space gradients:

# GET3D inference (requires the official NVIDIA GET3D repo)
# https://github.com/nv-tlabs/GET3D

import torch
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "GET3D")  # clone the repo first

from training.networks_get3d import GeneratorDMTetMesh

def load_get3d(ckpt_path: str, device: str = "cuda"):
    # Loads a GET3D generator from a pre-trained checkpoint.
    # Checkpoints available for ShapeNet cars, chairs, and motorbikes.
    G = GeneratorDMTetMesh(
        z_dim=512, c_dim=0, w_dim=512,
        img_resolution=1024, img_channels=3,
    ).to(device)
    ckpt = torch.load(ckpt_path, map_location=device)
    G.load_state_dict(ckpt["G_ema"])
    G.eval()
    return G

def sample_meshes(G, n: int = 4, device: str = "cuda") -> list:
    """Sample n textured meshes from the generator."""
    z_geo  = torch.randn(n, G.z_dim, device=device)
    z_tex  = torch.randn(n, G.z_dim, device=device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        result = G.generate_3d(
            z=z_geo, z_tex=z_tex,
            c=None, noise_mode="const",
        )
    # result["mesh_v"] : list of vertex tensors
    # result["mesh_f"] : list of face tensors
    # result["tex"]    : list of texture atlas images (1024×1024)
    return result
Mesh Post-Processing Pipeline
Raw generated meshes often need cleanup before use in a game engine or for 3D printing. A standard trimesh-based pipeline:

# pip install trimesh[easy] open3d
import trimesh
import trimesh.smoothing
import numpy as np

def postprocess_mesh(
    mesh: trimesh.Trimesh,
    target_faces: int = 10_000,
    smooth_iters: int = 5,
    smooth_lambda: float = 0.5,
) -> trimesh.Trimesh:
    """
    Standard post-processing: remove duplicates, fill holes,
    smooth, and decimate to a target face count.
    """
    # 1. Remove degenerate geometry
    mesh.remove_degenerate_faces()
    mesh.remove_duplicate_faces()
    mesh.remove_unreferenced_vertices()

    # 2. Fill small holes (optional — can add unwanted faces)
    trimesh.repair.fill_holes(mesh)

    # 3. Recompute normals consistently
    mesh.fix_normals()

    # 4. Laplacian smoothing — reduces marching-cubes staircase artefacts
    mesh = trimesh.smoothing.filter_laplacian(
        mesh, lamb=smooth_lambda, iterations=smooth_iters,
    )

    # 5. Quadratic error metric decimation
    mesh = mesh.simplify_quadratic_decimation(target_count=target_faces)

    # 6. Normalise to unit bounding sphere
    mesh.vertices -= mesh.centroid
    mesh.vertices /= mesh.scale    # scale = half bounding box diagonal

    print(f"Post-processed: {len(mesh.vertices)} verts, {len(mesh.faces)} faces")
    print(f"Watertight: {mesh.is_watertight}")
    return mesh


def uv_unwrap_and_bake(
    mesh: trimesh.Trimesh,
    texture_res: int = 1024,
) -> trimesh.visual.TextureVisuals:
    """
    Rough automatic UV unwrap using xatlas.
    pip install xatlas
    """
    import xatlas
    vmapping, indices, uvs = xatlas.parametrize(mesh.vertices, mesh.faces)
    # vmapping remaps vertices; indices are new faces.
    new_verts = mesh.vertices[vmapping]
    visual = trimesh.visual.TextureVisuals(
        uv=uvs,
        image=trimesh.visual.color.ColorVisuals().to_texture(
            resolution=(texture_res, texture_res)
        ).image,
    )
    new_mesh = trimesh.Trimesh(
        vertices=new_verts, faces=indices, visual=visual, process=False
    )
    return new_mesh


# Usage
raw_mesh = trimesh.load("raw_output.obj")
clean    = postprocess_mesh(raw_mesh, target_faces=5_000)
clean.export("clean_mesh.glb")   # GLB = binary glTF; best for web + game engines
Mesh Quality Metrics
Generated meshes are evaluated against ground-truth 3D CAD models using distance-based metrics:

import numpy as np
from scipy.spatial import cKDTree
import trimesh

def chamfer_distance(mesh_a: trimesh.Trimesh, mesh_b: trimesh.Trimesh,
                     n_samples: int = 10_000) -> float:
    """
    Chamfer Distance: average of nearest-neighbour distances in both directions.
    Lower is better. Invariant to mesh topology, only cares about surface points.
    """
    pts_a = mesh_a.sample(n_samples)
    pts_b = mesh_b.sample(n_samples)

    tree_b = cKDTree(pts_b)
    tree_a = cKDTree(pts_a)

    dist_ab, _ = tree_b.query(pts_a, k=1)   # a → nearest in b
    dist_ba, _ = tree_a.query(pts_b, k=1)   # b → nearest in a

    return (dist_ab.mean() + dist_ba.mean()) / 2


def earth_mover_distance_approx(mesh_a: trimesh.Trimesh, mesh_b: trimesh.Trimesh,
                                 n_samples: int = 2_048) -> float:
    """
    Earth Mover's Distance (Wasserstein-1) approximation.
    pip install pot (Python Optimal Transport)
    """
    import ot
    pts_a = mesh_a.sample(n_samples).astype(np.float64)
    pts_b = mesh_b.sample(n_samples).astype(np.float64)
    # Uniform weights
    w_a = np.ones(n_samples) / n_samples
    w_b = np.ones(n_samples) / n_samples
    M = ot.dist(pts_a, pts_b, metric="euclidean")
    return ot.emd2(w_a, w_b, M)


# Typical results on ShapeNet:
# Good method: CD ≈ 0.002 – 0.006 (normalised to unit sphere)
# Naïve baselines: CD > 0.02
Choosing the Right Pipeline
Scenario
Recommended
Why
Multi-photo of a real object → mesh
Nerfstudio + marching cubes
Best fidelity when you have 50+ images with good coverage
Single photo → game-ready mesh
InstantMesh
Best quality + UV texture from a single input image, ~50 s
Generative design (batch of shapes)
GET3D or Shap-E
~1 s inference per mesh, good diversity for a given shape class
Real-time render from multi-image input
3DGS + SuGaR
Real-time Gaussian render + optional mesh export with texture
Key Takeaways
Marching cubes converts any implicit scalar field (SDF, NeRF density) to a triangle mesh at a chosen iso-value; resolution controls the quality/memory trade-off (64³ → coarse, 512³ → fine).
SuGaR converts 3D Gaussian Splatting outputs to meshes by adding a surface regularisation term — the only way to get a proper mesh from a 3DGS scene.
InstantMesh (Zero123++ + LRM + FlexiCubes) is the best open-source single-image-to-mesh pipeline: ~50 s, UV-textured, Apache-2.0.
FlexiCubes improves on standard marching cubes by jointly optimising vertex positions during NeRF training, producing cleaner topology without post-processing.
Standard post-processing (degenerate removal, Laplacian smoothing, quadratic decimation, UV unwrap via xatlas) is always needed before a generated mesh is game-engine ready.
Chamfer Distance is the standard mesh quality metric: lower is better; good open-source methods achieve CD ≈ 0.002–0.006 on ShapeNet (normalised to unit sphere).
